reception.label: Reception staffing
reception.summary: Reception staffing analytics. Two layers: `get_reception_load` for the runtime picture (staff vs demand per 10-min bucket per side), and `get_pool_topology` / `get_side_policy` / `list_locations` for the configured relationships that shape it. When a location shows demand but zero rostered staff, that's a red flag — verify via pool-topology whether it should be shared, cross-check the roster with `get_roster_day`, and if still ambiguous ask the user rather than assume.

tools.description: Dashboard metrics for the practice: total queries, success rate, average response time, active users, last query timestamp. Takes `days` (default 30) for the lookback window. No PII. Useful for 'how is the system performing' or 'what's our query throughput'.

Fetch the appointment book (the schedule grid of all appointments across practitioners) for a given date and location. Returns rows with patient name/DOB/address/phones/reason (all REDACTED before you see them) plus the doctor name, slot time, and status. Use for questions like 'how busy is the Crace clinic today' or 'which appointments are running late' — but remember you cannot see patient identities; refer to them by internalId if you need to discuss a specific row.

List the practitioners rostered at a specific location today, with their department, shift times, and scheduled hours. Doctor/staff names ARE surfaced (not redacted — policy). Use this to answer 'who is working today' or 'which GPs are on at Lyneham this afternoon'.

Practice-wide aging summary: total debt, number of debtor patients, and number of unpaid invoices, broken down into buckets (0-30 days, 31-60, 61-90, 91-120, 121-180, 180+). No per-patient data — aggregate only. Optional `fromDate` and `minAmount` filters. Use for overview questions like 'what is our total outstanding AR' or 'how much is in the 180+ bucket.'

Fetch one invoice in full detail by its invoice ID. Returns line items, totals, payment status, provider, and the patient info. patientName is REDACTED; invoice details (amounts, line items, status, provider) come through cleanly. Use after list_recent_invoices or get_patient_invoices.

All invoices for one patient, organised into aging buckets (0-30 days, 30-60, etc.). Shows patient-level totalDebt, then per-bucket invoice count/total, then individual invoices with date, amount, age in days, provider name, and payer label (bulk-bill, private, DVA etc). patientName is REDACTED; the rest is non-PII. Requires the patient's internalId.

List patients with outstanding debt, paginated. Default 50 per page; use `page` and `pageSize` to page through. Filter by aging `bucket` (e.g. 0-30, 31-60, 61-90, 91-120, 121-180, 180+), `minAmount`, `assignedTo` (staff), `fromDate`, or `search` (matches patient name substring — the name itself still comes back REDACTED, but the filter works server-side). Each row gives the patientId, redacted patientName, totalDebt, invoiceCount, age of oldest invoice, which aging bucket it falls in, and current collection/case status. Use the patientId with get_patient_invoices, get_patient_appointments, or get_patient_by_id for follow-up. The top-level `totalCount` tells you how many debtors exist overall; if it exceeds `pageSize`, tell the user and ask if they want the next page or a narrower filter.

Recent invoices across the whole practice, most recent first. Returns up to `limit` rows (default 50). patientName REDACTED per row; invoice id, amount, date, status, provider visible. For practice-wide reporting questions ('what invoices were raised today', 'which providers have unpaid invoices this week').

Admin payment reconciliation report. One row per invoice in the date range with the full banking + Medicare/DVA picture: invoice total and status, Tyro transaction ref + MID/TID, banking batch + bank date + bank amount, payment method and reference, plus Medicare/DVA claim status and amounts paid. Requires admin role on the caller's JWT — non-admin users get 403. Use for reconciliation questions: 'which invoices weren't banked', 'reconcile batch X', 'Medicare claim status for April', 'what Dr Smith billed last week paid vs unpaid'. Dates MUST be ISO YYYY-MM-DD; unparseable dates are silently dropped upstream (SQL Server TRY_CONVERT). patientName is REDACTED; every other field is non-PII. Response is paginated — pageSize capped at 2000, default 50; honour totalCount to decide whether to page further.

fromDate.description: Start of date range, ISO YYYY-MM-DD. Inclusive.

toDate.description: End of date range, ISO YYYY-MM-DD. Inclusive.

tools.description: Fetch a patient's appointment history, split into `upcoming` and `past` lists. For each appointment: date/time, duration, appointment type, status, and the doctor's name (doctor names are NOT redacted — they're safe to surface). Requires the patient's internalId (from search_patients or get_patient_by_id). Use this to answer questions like 'when did Jane last see Dr Smith' or 'what upcoming appointments does she have'.

Fetch one patient record by their internal BP ID. All PII fields (names, DOB, address, phones, Medicare number, emails, displayName, formattedAddress) are REDACTED before you see them; only the internalId, gender, and non-identifying flags come through in the clear. Use the output to confirm you have the right patient before calling follow-up tools; do not try to reconstruct PII from what is visible.

Find patients by first name and/or surname (minimum 2 characters for each supplied term). Returns up to `maxResults` patient records (default 20, max 50). Patient PII (names, DOB, address, phones, Medicare number) is REDACTED before you see it; `internalId` is passed through unchanged and is what you should use to look up the patient in follow-up tools. IMPORTANT: if the number of rows returned equals `maxResults`, there may be more matching patients — tell the user and ask if they want to narrow the search (more specific names) or see additional results (up to 50).

Reception-staffing load snapshot for the requested location AND every location it pools with, on one date, at 10-minute-bucket resolution. Returns per-hour rollups AND the six 10-min buckets inside each hour. Each row exposes minutes available (rostered − breaks + manager coverage − Side Quest peels), minutes needed (appts + calls + overhead), load% (MAX across buckets — spikes not smoothed away), plus per-doctor check-in / check-out flow.

Pool expansion is automatic and topology-driven: when you query a leader (e.g. `Crace`), you also get its sharers' rows (`Crace4Kids` with `sharingTarget: 'Crace'`). When you query a sharer (`Crace4Kids`), you get both its own row and the leader it feeds into. Rows outside the requested location's pool are filtered out. Use `get_pool_topology` if you need the definitive relationship without a date-scoped call.

Status codes: `ok` / `tight` / `overload` = staffed pool with load in the healthy / warning / red range. `shared` = this row is a sharer whose demand is analysed on its leader (see sharingTarget). `closed` = zero rostered staff on this row; NOTE this does NOT mean zero demand — a `closed` row can still carry real `minutesForAppointments` and `minutesForCalls` from a booked schedule. That's the shape of unmet demand or informally-covered coverage; cross-reference `get_pool_topology` to see if it should be shared, and `get_roster_day` to see if any staff might informally cover it.

Multi-side locations (e.g. Crace with Rec 1 / Rec 2) emit one row per side keyed by `partKey` — do NOT sum sides for a location total, they represent independent pools. Pool sharers emit a `sharingTarget` field pointing at the leader whose row absorbed their demand.

Each bucket row also carries `proposedBreaks[]` — one entry per shift whose projected break window overlaps this 10-min bucket, listing the person, department, break window, and minutes attributed to this bucket. Sum reconciles to the aggregate `breakMinutes` on the same row. Empty on `closed` and `shared` rows (no rostered staff). Attribution already applies (a) the reception-department filter and (b) the part-attribution weighting, so entries won't show non-reception staff or wrong-side people.

Date is ISO (YYYY-MM-DD). No PII.

Every practice location the caller can see. Use as the starting point when you don't know a location's exact name — path params on the other reception tools (`get_reception_load`, `get_side_policy`, `get_roster_day`) are exact matches, case-sensitive. Returns id + name only; for pool relationships (which locations share with which) use `get_pool_topology`, for multi-side splits use `get_side_policy`, for staffing on a specific date use `get_roster_day`. No PII.

Configured pool mode for every location, as stored in the reception-departments table. Static, not date-dependent. Mode is `own` (self-pooling location like Crace, Lyneham) or `shared` (this location's reception minutes are absorbed by another leader — `sharedFromLocation` points at the target).

This reflects ONLY the DB-configured reception-department pooling mechanism — the one that drives get_reception_load's `sharingTarget` / `sharedFrom` fields. It does NOT capture: (a) informal pooling where receptionists physically walk between locations without a config row, (b) BP-side appointment-type routing that lands check-ins at a different desk, (c) same-manager coverage arrangements. If get_reception_load shows a location with appointment demand but zero rostered staff AND get_pool_topology says `own`, that's either a genuine staffing gap or informal-only pooling — do NOT assume it's covered; call it out or cross-check with get_roster_day. No PII.

How a location is split into sides (e.g. Crace → Rec 1 / Rec 2) and how doctors + reception departments are attributed to each side. Combines three underlying configs in one call:

- `sides`: partKey + displayName + check-in share % (each side is an independent pool for load-math purposes).
- `doctorSides`: doctor→side mapping — a doctor's appointment check-outs are attributed to their configured side.
- `departmentSides`: BP reception-department→side mapping — the dept a receptionist is rostered against determines which side their minutes belong to.

Empty `sides` list ⇒ location has no split configured, single-pool math applies (get_reception_load will emit one row per hour with partKey=null). Static config, not date-dependent — the sides / mappings don't change between days. Doctor names in `doctorSides.doctorDisplayName` are cached snapshots at mapping-write time; the authoritative name lives in BP. No PII.

Every shift rostered at a location on a specific date, from Tanda. Includes reception AND clinical staff — the filter is location + date, not role, so departmentName tells you what each shift is for. Each shift returns user id + name, department, start / finish times (ISO strings) + human-readable formatted times, hours scheduled, and acceptance status (`approved` / `pending`).

Use this to answer questions the reception-load calc can't reach on its own — e.g. 'is Crace4Kids covered by anyone physically today?' when `get_pool_topology` says no formal pooling exists (informal pooling shows up as roster overlap here, not config rows), or 'who could I move from Rec 1 to Rec 2 without breaking coverage?'. Also useful for verifying that a `closed`-status row in get_reception_load really does have zero staff (vs a config issue).

Empty response ⇒ no one rostered on that date at that location. Date is ISO (YYYY-MM-DD). Staff names ARE surfaced (not redacted — same policy as `list_practitioners_today`).

The load-model constants and appointment-type group percentages that drive the reception-load calc. Practice-wide singleton, not date-dependent. Use this to explain WHY a load% is what it is — without these Claude has to hand-wave the arithmetic.

Key fields grouped by purpose:

- Overhead & timing: `overheadMinutesPerHourPerReceptionist` (fixed admin time per rostered hour, subtracted from available), `callDurationMinutes` (minutes per expected call), `appointmentCheckInPaddingMinutes` / `appointmentCheckOutPaddingMinutes` (how many minutes before/after an appt is 'reception minutes').
- Break policy: `defaultBreakMinutesPerLongShift` + `longShiftHoursThreshold` (fallback when Tanda supplies no break data — only kicks in for shifts >= threshold hours AND when `breakSchedule` below is empty), `breakSchedule` (percentage-based break points; when non-empty this OVERRIDES the fallback pair, which becomes dead config).
- Appointment-type groups: `mode` (`by_type` / `blended`), four groups × three fields each (`groupNPercentOfAppts`, `groupNCheckInMin`, `groupNCheckOutMin`) and the computed `averageMinutesPerAppointment` blend.
- Provenance: `updatedBy` + `updatedAtUtc` — when config last changed.

No PII.

Every doctor with BP session data at a location (or any of its sharer children). Pool-aware — querying a leader (e.g. `Crace`) returns doctors sessioned at both Crace and any locations that share into it (like Crace4Kids); querying a sharer returns just that sharer's own doctors. Deduped by `doctorUserId`; case-insensitive location match against BP's `BpDoctorSessions.LocationName`. Use this to answer 'which doctors work at X' or to cross-reference with `get_side_policy.doctorSides` (which uses `doctorUserId` as the join key). Empty response ⇒ no session data synced yet OR no doctors mapped to this location. Names come from BP, may vary slightly from `get_side_policy.doctorSides.doctorDisplayName` (which is a manager-typed snapshot). No PII.

Full BP appointment-type catalogue with each type's configured group (1..4) — or `null` when it's falling back to `defaultGroup`. Reception-load math depends on this: an appointment of a given BP code lands in a group, and each group's `groupNCheckInMin` + `groupNCheckOutMin` from `get_settings` determine how many reception minutes that appointment contributes. Use when interpreting an appointment mix (e.g. 'why does this hour need so many reception minutes — what appointment types are booked?'); combine with `get_settings` for the minute weights. Static config, not date-dependent. No PII.
